text.title: The surface-coupling review (F001 → F002 lesson)
text.summary: Item 9 is the result of one specific historical failure. This file records the failure and the review protocol it triggered.

section.title: The empirical anchor
section.p: F001 in the faion plugin defined a skill trigger description. F002 later added an unrelated trigger description. The two descriptions overlapped at the keyword layer, so the auto-router fired the wrong skill for several user inputs. The bug was invisible at code-review because each PR only looked at its own diff. The fix was retrofitting an explicit surface-coupling review.

section.title: What counts as a public surface

list.item: Skill description / triggers (the auto-router keys off these).
API path or method signature exposed to clients.
CLI flag, subcommand, or environment variable consumed externally.
Public type/class/function name exported from a library boundary.
Database table or column name read by downstream systems.

rule.statement: For every public surface added or modified by the feature, grep the project for sibling surfaces in the same namespace and document — in readiness.md — that no collision exists OR that the collision is intentional and disambiguated.
rule.rationale: The collision is invisible at single-PR review and only surfaces at runtime. Forcing the explicit grep moves the check left.

example.note: Adding skill `sdd-batch-orchestrator` → grep `description:` across `skills/faion/workflows/` → find sibling `sdd-promotion-gate-checklist`. Verify keyword overlap is minimal; if not, narrow one description.

antipattern.description: Ticking item 9 because "this feature does not add a public surface" without naming what was checked.
antipattern.reason: The default answer to "any public surface?" is yes more often than people think — internal refactors expose types, CLI flags get added in passing. State what you checked, not just the conclusion.
